one_line_takeaway: THRD is a training-free, four-module inference-time framework that models risk as accumulating across conversation turns (rather than judging each turn in isolation), cutting multi-turn jailbreak ASR to roughly 0.2-4.0% against X-Teaming and Tempest on Qwen2.5-7B and Llama3-8B while keeping utility loss under 1.5 points, at the cost of ~15-22s per-turn latency and residual over-refusal (12-19%) driven mainly by keyword-sensitive turn-level scoring.
key_finding: On Table 1 (main results), THRD reduces ASR to 4.0%/1.3% (X-Teaming Harm/Adv) and 2.1%/0.5% (Tempest Harm/Adv) on Qwen2.5-7B, and to 1.6%/0.2% (X-Teaming) and 0.6%/1.0% (Tempest) on Llama3-8B, while degrading MMLU/GSM8K utility by at most 1.5 points (e.g., Qwen MMLU 82.0%->81.7%, GSM8K 92.9%->92.6%; Llama MMLU 72.4%->70.9%, GSM8K 83.4%->82.5%). By contrast, the single-turn baselines applied per-turn diverge sharply between attacks: PROACT holds Tempest ASR low (2.6%/3.1% on Qwen, 2.4%/2.5% on Llama) but is vulnerable to X-Teaming (63.5%/67.0% on Qwen, 47.4%/24.0% on Llama); SAGE fails catastrophically on Qwen against X-Teaming (86.2%/83.6% ASR) and incurs severe over-refusal (61.2% on Qwen, 99.6% on Llama XSTest) and utility loss (MMLU drops of 13.7 and 11.8 points). All three defenses (THRD, SAGE, PROACT) achieve 0% ASR against the single-turn attack AutoDAN (Table 2), showing THRD's temporal machinery does not regress single-turn robustness. Ablation (Table 3, X-Teaming/Llama3-8B/HarmBench) shows removing persistent rejection raises ASR from 1.59% to 5.15%; removing TRA or HCA individually causes the largest, comparable degradation (+23.78 and +24.48 points respectively); removing RE causes a smaller but substantial +15.48-point rise; removing both HCA and RE together gives the worst degradation (+31.71 points), though TRA alone still cuts ASR from the undefended 86.7% to 33.30%. Latency profiling (Table 4) shows HCA dominates per-turn cost -- 63.2% of THRD's ~15.2s total on Qwen and 77.7% of ~22.2s on Llama -- versus SAGE's 5.37s/8.27s. Shuffling conversation history before HCA (Table 5) raises ASR (Qwen 0.7%->1.3%, Llama 0.8%->1.2%), confirming HCA exploits temporal ordering, though shuffled ASR stays low, indicating content-level cues also matter. The Decision-formula ablation (Table 6) finds all 7 tested Lambda/Omega variant combinations keep ASR under 2%, with Linear-Decay + L2-Norm giving the most cross-architecture-stable result (0.57% Qwen, 0.85% Llama, 0.28-point gap). First-rejection-trigger analysis on X-Teaming shows only 18.0% (Qwen) / 29.1% (Llama) of attacks are caught at Turn 1, with the majority first caught at Turn 2 (55.8%/61.8%) and a smaller tail at Turn 3+ (26.2%/9.1%), i.e., over 70% of attacks require Turn 2 or later to detect. Over-refusal remains a residual weakness: THRD's best TRA prompt variant reaches 12.4% on Qwen (vs. 9.6% undefended baseline) and 18.8% on Llama, attributed mainly to TRA's sensitivity to individual sensitive keywords in benign queries rather than to the temporal-aggregation mechanism itself.
core_contribution: THRD is a training-free, inference-time defense framework against multi-turn jailbreak attacks on LLMs. It reframes multi-turn safety as a temporal risk-aggregation problem rather than a per-turn classification problem. It integrates four modules: a Turn-level Risk Assessor (TRA) that assigns an ordinal risk score R_i in [1,5] to the current input via a two-stage prompting scheme adapted from prior work (Ding et al. 2025a), with three new prompt variants the authors design to reduce keyword-triggered false positives; a Historical Context Analyzer (HCA) that analyzes the full prior conversation (conversational form, topic-sensitivity evolution, and attack-chain completeness) using a multi-stage semantic-reasoning framework adapted from ARMOR (Zhao et al. 2025c), producing a binary SAFE/UNSAFE judgment; a Response Evaluator (RE) that scores the previous model response along content safety, information sensitivity, and boundary compliance to detect facilitative (non-refusal but harm-enabling) outputs, triggered conditionally rather than every turn; and a Decision Module that combines these three signals into a time-evolving score F(i) = R_i + Lambda(R_i)*Omega(J_h, J_r), where Lambda is an attenuation function that down-weights historical signal influence as current-turn severity rises, and a trend-based penalty +delta is added when scores rise monotonically over three consecutive turns. Once a high-risk refusal is triggered, all subsequent turns are refused unconditionally (persistent/irreversible rejection). The paper's own novelty is explicitly scoped as the decomposition into three complementary temporal signals and their orchestration via this scoring formula, not the individual TRA/HCA analysis techniques, which are stated to be adapted from prior single-turn work (Ding et al. 2025a for TRA's two-stage paradigm, ARMOR for HCA's semantic-reasoning stages, and the discriminator-generator gap motivation from SDGO/Ding et al. 2025b for RE).
threat_model: Adversaries issue multi-turn conversations to a deployed LLM in which individual turns are each designed to appear benign (e.g., gradual escalation, role-play framing, decomposed/nested requests) while the accumulated conversational context steers the model toward producing harmful content that a single-turn safety filter would not catch. The paper explicitly targets two classes of state-of-the-art multi-turn attack strategies as its adversary: X-Teaming (Rahman et al. 2025), a multi-agent collaborative attack-planning framework using GPT-4o to generate attack strategies, and Tempest (Zhou and Arel 2025), a breadth-first tree-search attack with branch factor 5. AutoDAN (a single-turn optimization-based jailbreak) is also tested to confirm the defense does not regress on single-turn attacks. The defender is assumed to control an inference-time wrapper around the target LLM (no retraining/fine-tuning access needed) and can call an evaluator LLM to score each turn's input, conversation history, and the model's own prior response.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (Limitations section): (1) TRA, HCA, and RE are all prompt-based semantic judgments, so THRD's effectiveness is tied to the reasoning ability of the underlying evaluator model, and future attacks specifically optimized against THRD's prompting patterns or scoring dynamics could reduce its effectiveness (an adaptive-attacker threat is acknowledged but not evaluated). (2) Temporal dynamics are modeled via lightweight score aggregation rather than sequential/learned modeling, which may limit effectiveness against attacks that distribute intent across substantially longer trajectories than tested (evaluation caps at 7 turns for the trigger-turn analysis). (3) The persistent/irreversible rejection mechanism is intentionally conservative for security-critical deployment but may be overly restrictive for general-purpose systems, with no reversible or uncertainty-aware recovery path. (4) HCA is the primary latency bottleneck (63-78% of total per-turn overhead, ~15-22s per turn total), which the authors flag as needing context compression or module unification. Additional limitations evident from the results themselves: over-refusal on Llama3-8B remains elevated (18.8%) even with the best TRA variant; ablations for the decision-formula and history-shuffling analyses were only run on one attack/dataset combination (Tempest/AdvBench, 100 instructions) rather than across the full attack/model matrix; and safety judging relies on GPT-4o as an automated judge (with only spot manual-annotation verification), which is itself an imperfect proxy for ground-truth harm.

quantitative_results: THRD over-refusal (XSTest): 12.4% on Qwen (best TRA variant, vs 9.6% no-defense baseline), 18.8% on Llama (vs 16.0% no-defense baseline)
AutoDAN (single-turn) ASR: THRD, SAGE, and PROACT all achieve 0% on both models (vs. 76.9%/81.0% Qwen and 60.0%/75.0% Llama undefended)

Ablation on X-Teaming/Llama3-8B/HarmBench: full THRD 1.59% ASR; w/o Persistent Rejection 5.15% (+3.56); w/o TRA 25.37% (+23.78); w/o HCA 26.07% (+24.48); w/o RE 17.07% (+15.48); w/o HCA&RE 33.30% (+31.71)
Per-turn latency: THRD totals ~15.2s (Qwen2.5-7B) and ~22.2s (Llama3-8B), with HCA accounting for 9.60s/63.2% and 17.24s/77.7% respectively, vs. SAGE at 5.37s (Qwen) / 8.27s (Llama)
History-shuffling effect on ASR (Tempest/AdvBench): Qwen 0.7%->1.3% shuffled; Llama 0.8%->1.2% shuffled

Decision-formula ablation (Tempest, 100 AdvBench instructions): best combo Linear Decay + L2 Norm gives 0.57% (Qwen) / 0.85% (Llama), smallest cross-architecture gap of 0.28 points; worst-generalizing combos include No Decay (0.32% Qwen vs 1.91% Llama) and Weighted Sum (0.25% Qwen vs 1.53% Llama)
First-rejection-trigger distribution on X-Teaming: Turn 1 catches 18.0% (Qwen)/29.1% (Llama); Turn 2 catches 55.8%/61.8%; Turn 3+ catches 26.2%/9.1% -- over 70% require Turn 2 or later

Selected decision thresholds: tau_low=2.0, tau_high=3.5, chosen from a threshold-sensitivity sweep where over-refusal drops from near 100% at threshold 1.0 to under 20% at threshold 2.0 on both models
baselines_compared: PROACT (Zhao et al. 2025b, single-turn inference-time defense applied per-turn)
SAGE (Ding et al. 2025a, single-turn inference-time defense applied per-turn)
